chapters.title: Coming to terms with the receding curtains of life
chapters.text:
Death isn’t what scares people when they age. Instead, they’re frightened of those things that plague the moments right before their last breath — loss of hearing, memory decline, saying goodbye to best friends, and an erosion of their way of life. Felix Silverstone calls old age a succession of losses.

Old age is not a battle. Old age is a massacre. ~ Philip Roth

Our longevity largely depends on luck and quality of care, which includes food, exercise, healthy levels of blood pressure, and proper health care. Regardless, losses snowball until we can no longer go through life independently. For the most part, death is no longer sudden — most people live for years, too impaired to survive without someone else’s support.

For most seniors, long life is synonymous with a reversed order of family dependence.

Alice Hobson, who was Gawande’s then-girlfriend’s granny, stayed alone in her later years; her husband had died after a heart attack. She dreaded few things more than the thought of leaving her home. Hobson’s residence was where she felt well-adjusted, coordinated, and had an adequate grasp of her life. But on one occasion, she fell victim to some men attacking her on the street. It was obvious that she wasn’t safe on her own anymore. Her son decided it was best to find a place where Alice could thrive.

In Europe and the United States, nursing homes date back centuries. Still, admitting you require assistance is no easy feat. Alice hesitated for a long time before agreeing to move there. Dependency was hard to accept, but she knew she hadn’t much time left. Hobson's children later committed her to a high-brow retirement home called Longwood House, and they occasionally visited her.

During a visit, she whispered, “I’m ready” in her son’s ear. Then, on an April night, Hobson developed abdominal pains. She decided against pressing the call button or getting her roommate’s attention. She chose to stay in bed. In the morning, the aides who came to wake up the residents found Alice dead. She wasn’t afraid anymore and accepted her fate willingly.

chapters.title: In pursuit of an independent life
chapters.text:
Recently, older adults seem less emotional about their children leaving to start their own lives. Historians believe the older generations are better off economically and seldom unhappy going about the rest of their lives in relative seclusion.

The rapid growth of the economy changed the real estate ownership model. While elderly parents used to pass down land to their grown children, now they have a few more options. They can either sell or rent it out, and this switch allows for a more leisurely and enjoyable retirement.

Advancements in the healthcare field are also a significant factor in this shift. Several centuries ago, life expectancy was much shorter. Nowadays, parents can see their children grow into adults before worrying about the limitations of old age. Their relationships change — parents tend to lose the position of financial stability providers at this stage. Most importantly, once their offspring start building their own lives, parents have a chance to live for themselves.

Humans of all ages crave independence. The trick is being ready to accept the responsibility that comes with it.

On the other hand, this way of living comes with a downside. The impending realities of life don't factor in our need for independence. Infirmities and serious ailments often set in, and fading health becomes unavoidable as time ceaselessly grinds away.

For example, let’s look at Alice’s story. At 84, she was in good health except for false teeth and eye surgery. She lived an active life, but then came a change. She became forgetful, which her family brushed off as simple mistakes. Then, her friend noticed some bruises on her leg. It turned out that she’d fallen the night before. The falls started to happen frequently. After several visits to the doctor, it became apparent that age was taking its toll on Alice, and there was no way to fix it. Her independence was fading away, and there was no way for her to control it.

A question then becomes apparent: what use is the autonomy we struggle to attain all of our productive lives if we cannot sustain it in the end?

chapters.title: The natural degenerative tendencies of health
chapters.text:
In theory, humans could have had an immortal and healthy life without a single problem on the horizon. But, of course, we are prone to illness and injury. When these issues strike, the world seems to cave in.

Mortality is a perplexing subject. Over the years, many debates, speculations, and controversial opinions have existed around it. This discourse led to accepting that aging connects to unpredictable, inevitable wear and tear over a number of years.

In recent years, however, aging has been linked to genetics. While studying nematodes — species of worms —researchers managed to increase their lifespan using gene modification twofold. However, despite clear evidence, some still believe that the aging process isn’t programmed in our genes; after all, the lifespan of humans climbed from thirty to eighty years.

Modern medicine allows people to overcome even fatal diseases like cancer. Treatment can prolong a patient’s lifespan for years. However, the inevitable demise may come suddenly — in months or weeks, whether due to illness or old age.

Decline remains an unavoidable fate, so focus on what you can control instead — leading a fulfilling life.

But while our internal gear is still wound and running, medical science can influence and chart the course of our health, allowing us to put off the mortal doomsday a little longer and helping a person retain abilities that matter most to them.

An excellent example of such preservation was Felix Silverstone, a geriatrician at the Parker Jewish Institute in New York. The remarkable thing about Silverstone was his control over his life.

He kept working well into his eighties, even after surviving a heart attack. At the time when he began his career, no one would believe that an eighty-year-old person with such an extensive medical record would remain so active. Not only was he a contributing scholar, but he was also the primary caregiver to his wife. At 87, Silverstone still drove while running errands to pick up his wife’s medications; he cared for her until she passed.

Atul Gawande asked Felix if it was possible to envision the aging process and make it predictable. He replied that there is no reproducible path of aging. Even though medical research has extended our lifespan, we still fall apart in unpredictable ways. Therefore, the approach to aging and mortality will differ for each individual.

Did you know? In 1790, people over 65 comprised a mere 2% of the American population, whereas today, this number has risen to 14%. In Japan, Italy, and Germany, the elderly population is over 20%.

chapters.title: Dependency is inevitable in old age
chapters.text:
Death isn’t what scares people when they age. Instead, they’re frightened of those things that plague the moments right before their last breath — loss of hearing, memory decline, saying goodbye to best friends, and an erosion of their way of life. Hobson’s residence was where she felt well-adjusted, coordinated, and had an adequate grasp of her life. But on one occasion, she fell victim to some men attacking her on the street. chapters.title: Understanding and adjusting to the impending realities of death
chapters.text:
Sara Thomas Monopoli, a patient at Gawande’s hospital, was pregnant with her first child when doctors informed her of the grim reaper right around the corner; she was just 34. The first symptoms she developed were cough and back pain. An X-ray showed her lung collapsed and her chest filled up with fluid. Her vital signs indicated the early signs of infection; however, it turned out to be lung cancer lodged deep into the lining of Monopoli’s chest.

The fetus was thirty-nine weeks old, and the specialist had to break the news to her as soon as possible. Monopoli’s husband and parents were present when they informed her of the discovery.

The diagnosis was the moment in Monopoli’s story that posed a riddle that everyone living in this era of modern medicine ought to ponder: what could Sara and her doctors have done at that point?

Before the advent of modern medicine, dying was largely spontaneous and unmetered. Hence, questions like these posed no problem. However, some diseases and conditions had a drawn-out natural history — with tuberculosis as a classic example.

Advances in modern medicine gifted us with knowledge, and we must use it to secure our lives better.

With most medical practitioners, discussions about terminal illness aim to determine what course of action the individual or their family would like to take. Serious diseases like cancer make the patient consider not only prolonging their life, but avoiding pain, staying emotionally connected to their loved ones, and coming to terms with the possibility of near death. The healthcare system often fails to address this complex combination of issues. Not to mention, the cost of treatment can be detrimental to the family’s budget. Terminally ill patients need professionals to help them prepare for what lies ahead.

Did you know? Second-order desires are about larger goals, like feeling happy and connecting with loved ones. Doctors must address them as much as first-order ones concerning the body.

chapters.title: Courage is the key to coping with an impending end
chapters.text:
A monumental transformation is occurring. We live in a time of change, and aging is no exception. People no longer have to spend their last days living miserably in a nursing home and will gladly agree to alternatives. But this is a period plagued with a lack of consensus on several issues formerly judged by nature. We moved away from the old way of aging and dying but haven’t found a new one yet. So, we remain stuck in between.

An undeniable advantage of the old system was its pseudo-automated method of decision-making about dying. You tried out the treatment suggesting the greatest potential for a positive outcome, and that was the only option. Back then, choosing between several available options and liaising with your healthcare provider to choose treatment accordingly was downright stressful and wasteful due to scarce resources and workforce. Indeed, this could only be the case when you have an expert to help you understand the implication of each highlighted option. Now, people have more autonomy over their last months or years.

Life choices may be challenging, but requesting and accepting help allows you to come out undefeated.

Courage is what people need most in the face of illness and aging. There are two types of it in such a situation. First is the courage to face reality and accept the truth, no matter how frightening. This variant of courage is taxing enough, and there are always multiple excuses to deviate from it. However, the second kind is the most daunting — the courage to know the truth and take action. You’d think uncertainty would be paralyzing, but knowing the outcome can pose an even bigger question.

Courage is strength in the face of knowledge of what is to be feared or hoped. Wisdom is prudent strength. ~ Atul Gawande, MD

The hard truth is that we cannot control how our lives end, but we can take action to improve the experience on an institutional level. We must understand that older people have more to worry about than living for as long as possible.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
It may be too ambitious to suggest that endings are controllable. Hardly anyone has total control over how their life pans out. The crises of physics, biology, and accidents are ultimately unavoidable in every individual’s life, but we are far from helpless.

A few conclusions become apparent when people accept society’s failings in treating the sick and aged. The loss often extends to not adjudging priorities beyond safety and longer life; we should give seniors a chance to shape their story concerning the very essence of life. It is expedient, and we have an opportunity to reshape the conversation around death and the years leading up to it.

Medical science has availed a large chunk of humanity with the remarkable power to tweak the natural limits of human life. But failure to recognize that medicine has limited ability causes much damage.

Medical professionals have been largely skewed about their job concerning the science of death. Healthcare’s ultimate goal is more than guarding health and warranting survival. It also ensures their patients' well-being, so they can worry about their reasons to live. This well-being is vital both in later years and throughout people’s lives.

Try this

Help yourself and your older friends and family to get the best out of life’s finitude by doing the following exercises:

• Occasionally spend time with older people. Listen to their stories, and try seeing life from their perspective.
• Learn more about the importance of senior healthcare.
• Actively and psychologically prepare yourself for physical decline in later years.

Read it till the end?
